title:
Background Jobs and Secur


  lp[j;aserf]ity Hardening Foundation
purpose: Establish the next-phase operational foundation for the parent backend: request ID propagation, worker entrypoint scaffolding that reuses already-built services, and lightweight security-control primitives for rate limiting and audit hooks on sensitive flows. This spec intentionally does not implement admin dashboards, broad asynchronous infrastructure, new database schema, duplicated Pay logic, or every future worker listed in the architecture docs.

context.summary: The roadmap and implementation plan both point to background jobs and security hardening as the next workstream after the completed Discord integration slice. Repo reality shows the application layer is now broad enough that the next useful step is operational hardening rather than another user-facing domain: there is an empty `app/workers/` package, request IDs are already part of the standard error DTO but are never assigned by middleware, existing services already encapsulate Pay projection refresh and reward event ingestion logic that workers can reuse, and there is no current rate-limiting or audit-hook foundation for high-risk browser actions.
repo_reality_summary: `IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md` now points to `specs/background_jobs_and_security_hardening.json` as the active workstream, but older narrative text in the plan still needs to be kept aligned with the new operational-hardening phase during planning runs.

Worker scaffolding is effectively absent today: `app/workers/__init__.py` exists only as an empty package marker, and repo searches found no `BackgroundTasks`, middleware-driven job dispatch, or concrete worker modules for Pay sync, email, attachment processing, announcement publishing, Discord sync, or stale session cleanup.

Operational worker-ready service logic already exists and should be reused instead of rebuilt in workers: `app/services/pay_projection_service.py` already owns Pay refresh and safe fallback behavior, and `app/services/reward_event_ingestion_service.py` already orchestrates trusted reward event ingestion with idempotency checks against `RewardProgressionRepository`.

Security/error groundwork is partial rather than complete: `app/api/errors.py` and `app/api/exception_handlers.py` already support `request_id` in the standard error envelope via `request.state.request_id`, but `app/main.py` registers no middleware that actually assigns a request ID or returns one to clients.

Repo searches found no rate-limiting implementation, no audit logging abstractions, and no existing logging helpers under `app/`; high-risk surfaces such as auth routes, Discord OAuth callback routes, billing mutation routes, and support ticket creation are present but currently unguarded by shared operational-control primitives.

Session lifecycle primitives already exist in the auth slice: `app/db/repositories/auth_repository.py` and `app/services/auth_service.py` provide session listing and revocation behavior, which gives the future build agent a realistic base for stale-session cleanup without reopening auth domain rules or adding new schema.

Relevant regression coverage already exists around the application foundation and adjacent reusable services in `tests/unit/test_application_layer_foundation.py`, `tests/unit/test_api_error_contract.py`, `tests/unit/test_pay_projection_service.py`, `tests/unit/test_reward_event_ingestion_service.py`, `tests/unit/test_auth_service.py`, and `tests/unit/test_config.py`, but there are no tests for request-ID middleware, worker entrypoints, rate-limit primitives, or audit-hook wiring.

global_guardrails: Do not expand scope beyond this spec.
Do not add dependencies unless John explicitly approves them.
Do not duplicate Pay commercial business rules in parent.
Do not store sensitive payment details in parent.
Do not build admin dashboards unless explicitly scoped.
Do not redesign stable frontend pages.
Do not make Discord linkage affect rewards or product access unless the spec explicitly says so.
Do not allow frontend APIs to directly award points/rewards/badges.
Do not return raw ORM objects from routers.
Do not mark incomplete items complete.
Append progress entries to the absolute end of `progress/progress.txt`.
Run `python -m compileall app tests alembic` before marking implementation items complete.
Run `docker compose -f docker-compose.test.yml up --build --abort-on-container-exit --exit-code-from test` before marking implementation items complete.

Keep this workstream dependency-free and foundation-oriented: prefer middleware, config, small helper modules, and service-backed workers over introducing Celery, Redis, task queues, or third-party rate-limit packages.
Workers must call existing services or narrowly scoped worker-facing services; do not duplicate Pay projection logic, reward progression rules, or auth policy inside worker modules.
Audit hooks and rate-limit details must avoid logging secrets, raw OAuth codes, session tokens, password-reset tokens, Discord user IDs, or sensitive support payload contents.
Do not add new database models or Alembic migrations unless repo reality proves a tiny support correction is absolutely required, and document any such correction explicitly before implementation.

items.title: Add Request ID Middleware and Error Propagation Foundation
items.description: Implement a small request-context foundation so every browser request receives a request ID that is attached to `request.state`, made available to the existing standard error contract, and exposed in a consistent response header where appropriate. Keep the change middleware-only and avoid redesigning route payloads.
acceptance_criteria: `app/main.py` or a small helper module registers request-ID middleware for the FastAPI app without adding third-party dependencies.
Requests that reach exception handlers now carry a non-empty request ID, and standard error responses include that `request_id` consistently instead of always omitting it.
Normal successful responses expose the request ID through a stable response header or equivalent minimal tracing surface documented in code/tests.
The implementation does not change the existing error envelope shape beyond populating `request_id`, and it does not require route-by-route edits across the application.

items.title: Introduce Worker Entrypoint Scaffolding Around Existing Services
items.description: Create the first concrete worker modules under `app/workers/` by wrapping already-existing service logic for Pay projection refresh, trusted reward event processing, and stale session cleanup. Keep workers thin, idempotent where possible, and callable from tests without requiring an external queue or scheduler.
acceptance_criteria: Concrete worker modules exist for at least Pay projection sync, reward event processing, and stale session cleanup, and each delegates to existing services or narrowly scoped service helpers instead of reimplementing business logic.
Worker entrypoints are pure enough to be invoked directly in unit tests and do not assume Celery, Redis, or any external job runner is already installed.
Session cleanup behavior is limited to expired or revoked-session maintenance using the existing auth repository/service foundation; it does not reopen login policy or session cookie design.
Pay-related workers only refresh or reconcile parent projections and do not mutate Pay commercial truth locally.
Reward workers only use trusted internal commands and preserve the existing idempotency pattern already present in `RewardEventIngestionService`.

items.title: Add Lightweight Rate-Limit and Audit-Hook Primitives for Sensitive Flows
items.description: Introduce dependency-free security-control primitives that the app can apply to high-risk operations without reopening every route in the system. The initial slice should cover configuration, reusable rate-limit checks, and audit-hook interfaces for sensitive auth, Discord callback, billing mutation, and support ticket creation flows while keeping PII handling strict.
acceptance_criteria: A reusable, configuration-backed rate-limit primitive exists in the app codebase and can be applied to at least the highest-risk browser actions called out in the security docs: login, signup, forgot password, resend verification, Discord OAuth callback, billing action initiation, and support ticket creation.

The initial implementation remains dependency-free and does not require Redis or a new persistence layer; if an in-memory limiter is used, tests and code comments must state its phase-1 operational limitation explicitly.

A small audit-hook abstraction exists for sensitive actions so auth, billing-action initiation, integration-link changes, or worker runs can emit safe audit events without logging raw secrets or sensitive payload bodies.
This item does not attempt to ship a full SIEM pipeline, admin audit UI, or comprehensive security event store.

items.title: Lock Operational Foundation Regression Coverage
items.description: Add focused regression tests that prove the request-ID middleware, worker entrypoints, and rate-limit/audit foundations exist and are wired through the application layer without broad route rewrites or silent DTO regressions.
acceptance_criteria: Unit tests cover request-ID assignment and propagation into the standard error contract.
Unit tests cover worker entrypoint behavior for Pay projection sync, reward event processing, and stale session cleanup using direct invocation rather than an external scheduler.
Unit tests cover rate-limit primitive behavior, configuration loading, and audit-hook safety expectations for sensitive flows.
Existing foundation/config tests are updated only where necessary to reflect the new operational surface; no unrelated domain test inventories are duplicated.

items.title: Verify Background Jobs and Security Hardening Foundation
items.description: Run the required verification suite after the operational foundation changes are implemented and only mark this spec complete when the request-ID, worker, and security-control slice is fully verified.
acceptance_criteria: `python -m compileall app tests alembic` passes.
`docker compose -f docker-compose.test.yml up --build --abort-on-container-exit --exit-code-from test` passes.
Operational-foundation tests cover request-ID propagation, worker entrypoints, and rate-limit or audit-control behavior.
Only fully completed items are marked complete, and `progress/progress.txt` is appended at EOF.

completion_definition: Relevant tests are added or updated for request-ID propagation, worker entrypoints, and lightweight security-control primitives.
No unrelated dirty files are introduced.
No incomplete item is marked complete.
